coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent distinguishing flavonoid glycosides against aglycones based on the selective recognization of boric acid-functional Eu(III)-organic framework
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Shuyun
Sun, Kunming
Liu, Shuyi
Wang, Qian
Fan, Jiatong
Zhao, Xian-En
Xu, Guobao
coredata.dc:description: Flavonoid glycosides are formed by dehydration condensation of aglycones and sugar molecules. Therefore, discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from their corresponding aglycones is a challenging task because they contain the same aglycone part in their molecular structures. Herein, boric acid-functional Eu(III)-organic framework (BA-Eu-MOF) was applied to discriminate flavonoid glycosides including baicalin (Bai), wogonoside (Wog), rutin (Rut), puerarin (Pue), quercitrin (Que) and astragalin (Ast) from their corresponding aglycones for the first time. Besides as organic ligand to sensitize the luminescence of Eu3+ through “antenna” effect, 5-boronobenzene-1,3 dicarboxylic acid provided recognition site for flavonoid glycosides. Infrared, fluorescence, UV–vis, and mass spectra were used to investigate the recognition reaction between BA-Eu-MOF and flavonoid glycosides. The data indicated that the cis-diols of flavonoid glycosides from sugars covalently bonded to boric acid group to form cyclic boronic esters, which quenched the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF at 620 nm through decreasing the intersystem efficiency, inner filter effect and photoelectron transfer. In contrast, aglycones could not alter the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF because of no covalent bond between them. This probe exhibited high sensitivity towards flavonoid glycosides with the low detection limits of 3.3 nM, 3.5 nM, 33 nM, 56 nM, 5.1 nM and 5.5 nM for Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut, respectively. The unique recognition ability of boric acid group enables selective and sensitive detection of flavonoid glycosides without the interference of their corresponding aglycones.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Flavonoid glycosides
Cis-diols
Cyclic boronic ester
Metal-organic framework

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of flavonoid glycosides in real samples
Discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from their aglycones
Detection of flavonoid glycosides with BA-Eu-MOF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), TEM image (B), FT-IR spectrum (C) and the full XPS spectrum (D) of BA-Eu-MOF.
Fluorescence emission spectra of BA-Eu-MOF (0.05 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of six flavonoid glycosides (50 μM) (A) and their corresponding aglycones (50 μM) (B).
The effects of reaction time (A), pH (B) and MOF amounts (D) on the fluorescence quenching efficiency. (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of BA-Eu-MOF with different amounts.
Linear relationships between (F–F 0)/F 0 and concentrations of Bai (A), Que (B), Wog (C), Ast (D), Pue (E) and Rut (F).
The (F–F 0)/F 0 of different substances towards BA-Eu-MOF. The concentration of each substance is 50 μM unless stated otherwise.

(A) The fluorescence excitation spectrum of BA-Eu-MOF and the UV–vis spectra of six flavonoid glycosides. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of BA-Eu-MOF in the absence and presence of six flavonoid glycosides. (C) HOMO and LUMO energies for 5-bop and six flavonoid glycosides.
BA-Eu-MOF used for discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from aglycones.
Comparison of flavonoid glycoside sensors based on various materials.
Detection of Bai (samples 1–3), Que (samples 4–6), Wog (samples 7–9), Ast (samples 10–12) and Pue (samples 13–15) in human blood samples and rutin (samples 16–18) in commercial rutin tablet.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent distinguishing flavonoid glycosides against aglycones based on the selective recognization of boric acid-functional Eu(III)-organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Catalytic Conversion and Clean Energy in Universities of Shandong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City, 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Catalytic Conversion and Clean Energy in Universities of Shandong Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flavonoid glycosides are formed by dehydration condensation of aglycones and sugar molecules. Therefore, discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from their corresponding aglycones is a challenging task because they contain the same aglycone part in their molecular structures. Herein, boric acid-functional Eu(III)-organic framework (BA-Eu-MOF) was applied to discriminate flavonoid glycosides including baicalin (Bai), wogonoside (Wog), rutin (Rut), puerarin (Pue), quercitrin (Que) and astragalin (Ast) from their corresponding aglycones for the first time. Besides as organic ligand to sensitize the luminescence of Eu3+ through “antenna” effect, 5-boronobenzene-1,3 dicarboxylic acid provided recognition site for flavonoid glycosides. Infrared, fluorescence, UV–vis, and mass spectra were used to investigate the recognition reaction between BA-Eu-MOF and flavonoid glycosides. The data indicated that the cis-diols of flavonoid glycosides from sugars covalently bonded to boric acid group to form cyclic boronic esters, which quenched the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF at 620 nm through decreasing the intersystem efficiency, inner filter effect and photoelectron transfer. In contrast, aglycones could not alter the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF because of no covalent bond between them. This probe exhibited high sensitivity towards flavonoid glycosides with the low detection limits of 3.3 nM, 3.5 nM, 33 nM, 56 nM, 5.1 nM and 5.5 nM for Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut, respectively. The unique recognition ability of boric acid group enables selective and sensitive detection of flavonoid glycosides without the interference of their corresponding aglycones.

Boric acid (BA)-functional Eu(III)-organic framework (BA-Eu-MOF) was applied to discriminate flavonoid glycosides including baicalin (Bai), wogonoside (Wog), rutin (Rut), puerarin (Pue), quercitrin (Que) and astragalin (Ast) from their corresponding aglycones for the first time.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As predominant form of natural flavonoids, flavonoid glycosides have aroused increasing interest due to their broad range physiological functions and biological activities [1]. For instance, baicalin (Bai) and wogonoside (Wog) have been reported to exhibit anti-bacteria, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, lowering blood pressure and antioxidant effects [2,3]. Rutin (Rut) has various pharmaceutical effects such as antivirus, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antitumor, antiarrhythmia, protective and expanding blood vessel [4]. Puerarin (Pue) has been used to treat coronary heart disease, cerebrovascular diseases, and diabetes owing to its antihyperglycemic effect and hepatoprotective activity [5]. Quercitrin (Que) and astragalin (Ast) exhibit strong antioxidant activity and anti-inflammatory activity, respectively [6,7]. Monitoring the levels of flavonoid glycosides in human biological fluids is necessary to understand their pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics for further investigating efficacy and designing rational dosage regiments, which requires sensitive analytical methods. However, these flavonoid glycosides are formed by dehydration condensation of their corresponding aglycones and sugar molecules (Fig. S1). Therefore, discrimination of glycosides from their corresponding aglycones is a challenging task because they contain the same aglycone part in their molecular structures [8].

Besides aglycones, flavonoid glycosides contain sugar molecules with typical cis-diols structure, which may become a key factor to discriminate them from their corresponding aglycones. Boronate affinity materials have become a powerful medium for recognizing 1,2- or 1,3-diols to form reversible covalent complex [9–13]. Because fluorescence methods possess the advantages of simple and fast operation, time-saving, and high sensitivity [14–16], a series of fluorescence sensors have been constructed to detect glucose with cis-diols structure based on boric acid-functionalized carbon dots [17], graphene quantum dots [18] and CdTe quantum dots [19]. However, the ortho hydroxyl groups on phenyl from some aglycones like quercetin and baicalein can also form cyclic boronic esters with boric acid [20–22], which will be one of the major obstacles for discriminating flavonoid glycosides from their aglycones. We are inspired to seek boric acid-functionalized materials for only bonding with cis-diols from sugars selectively but not response to sugars.

Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) have emerged as a unique luminescent material due to their large Stokes shift, high quantum yields and undisturbed emissive energy [23–26]. The development of functional Ln-MOFs with recognition site is one of the most significant achievements in Ln-MOFs-based sensors [27,28]. So, it is quite prospective to fabricate functional Ln-MOFs for specific recognition. In comparison with post-synthetic modification, one-step “synthesis-modification integration” is a simple but effective strategy to fabricate functionalized Ln-MOFs [29–31]. Thus, it is a key factor to select an organic ligand not only sensitizing Ln ions luminescence but also possessing the desired functional recognition sites. As functional ligand, 5-boronoisophthalic acid (5-bop) was used to synthesize functional Eu-MOFs for sensing Hg2+ [32], H2O2 [33], dopamine [34], F− [35,36] and gallic acid [37] owing to the high affinity of boric group towards these targets. However, no report has been found to detect flavonoid glycosides with boric acid-functionalized Ln-MOFs. Notably, boric acid-functionalized Eu-MOF (BA-Eu-MOF) cannot respond to saccharides because of the lack of sufficient electronic or energy changes, although saccharides can coordinate with boric acid group [33]. Aglycones part of flavonoid glycosides hold promising possibility of energy transfer between Ln-MOFs and flavonoid glycosides owing to the presence of benzene ring and phenolic hydroxyl group, thus inducing the fluorescence changes of Ln-MOFs.

Herein, we compounded BA-Eu-MOF using Eu3+ as the metal node and 5-bop as the ligand via a one-pot process for discriminating flavonoid glycosides from their corresponding aglycones. As shown in Scheme 1 , cis-diols of flavonoid glycosides from sugars covalently bonded to hydroxyl group of boric acid group to form cyclic boronic esters, which quenched the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF at 620 nm through decreasing the intersystem efficiency, inner filter effect (IFE) and photoelectron transfer (PET). In contrast, aglycones could not alter the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF because aglycones could not covalently bond to boric acid group owing to the lack of sugar molecules. Therefore, BA-Eu-MOF-based fluorescence strategy can be established for selective recognition of flavonoid glycosides to shield the interferences of their corresponding aglycones. The specific recognition ability of boric acid group towards flavonoid glycosides enables the development of promising sensors for other flavonoid glycosides.

Europium (III) chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), 5-boronobenzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid (5-bop), baicalin (Bai), puerarin (Pue), quercetin, wogonin, kaempferol, daidzein, d-glucose (Glu), glucuronic acid, rhamnose, amino acids, N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) and were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Rutin (Rut) purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Wogonoside (Wog), quercitrin (Que) and astragalin (Ast) were provided Weikeqi Bio-Technology, Co. Ltd. (Sichuan, Chian). Baicalein was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Rutinose was obtained from Acmec Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The distilled water was provided by Watson (Guangzhou, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed with Philips CM200-FEG. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were performed on a Sigma 300 field emission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out with Nicoletis 20 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a X pert3 X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with recorded on a ESCALAB 250xi spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were obtained on time-correlated single-photon counting unit of a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. UV–vis spectra were obtained with a UV3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). An Ultimate 3000 ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA) coupled with an AB SCIEX Triple Quad 5500 mass spectrometer (SCIEX Inc., Framingham, MA) was performed. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was performed with Agilent 1260 HPLC (Agilent Co., USA).

According to the literature [35], BA-Eu-MOF was synthesized as follows: EuCl3·6H2O (0.0366 g) and 5-bop (0.0210 g) were mixed with 10 mL DMF/H2O (V:V = 7:3) together followed by vigorous stirring for 2 h. Then, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifugated and washed with DMF and ethanol three times. After dried at 60 °C for 12 h, the white product was collected and stored for use.

50 μL BA-Eu-MOF (0.5 mg/mL) aqueous solution and 10 μL flavonoid glycosides with varied concentration were added into 440 μL phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH = 7.5). After incubation for 5 min, the emission spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of flavonoid glycosides in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood samples were provided by healthy adults from Qufu People's Hospital. Human blood samples were incubated with acetonitrile for 10 min followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min to eliminate the protein interference. The supernatant was spiked with known amounts of standard flavonoid glycosides solutions and diluted 20 times with distilled water before analysis. 20 μL of the above solution and 50 μL BA-Eu-MOF (0.5 mg/mL) aqueous solution were added into 430 μL PBS buffer (pH = 7.5). After incubation for 5 min, the emission spectra were recorded.

Rutin tablet was ground into powder. A certain amount of powder was weighted and dissolved with 30 mL distilled water. After filtration, a stock solution of 1 mM rutin was obtained, which was then performed fluorescence detection.

Firstly, SEM (Fig. 1 A) and TEM (Fig. 1B) were used to characterize the morphology of BA-Eu-MOF. The obtained BA-Eu-MOF exhibited regular nanorods with an average diameter of 100 nm, which was well matched with the reported literature [35]. To confirm the preparation of BA-Eu-MOF, FT-IR spectra of 5-bop and BA-Eu-MOF were recorded, respectively (Fig. 1C). The peak at 1699 cm−1 corresponding to the CO stretching vibration of ligand disappeared in BA-Eu-MOF, demonstrating the coordination of carboxylic group with Eu3+. The peaks at 1375 cm−1 and 1314 cm−1 corresponding to the B–O absorption were observed in both 5-bop and BA-Eu-MOF [35,38], demonstrating the successful preparation of BA-Eu-MOF. The XRD patterns of the obtained BA-Eu-MOF agreed well with the simulated Eu-MOF 2 (Fig. S2), which was synthesized using Eu3+ and isophthalic acid as metal node and organic ligand, respectively. The results indicate that boric group does not complex with Eu3+ and holds great potential to recognize target analyte as a free site [35]. Furthermore, the XPS of BA-Eu-MOF showed that B element existed as B 1s (Fig. 1D), further proving the freedom of boric acid group.

The similar absorption curves of BA-Eu-MOF with ligand demonstrated the main attribution of π-π∗ transition of 5-bop (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence spectra of BA-Eu-MOF at different excitation wavelengths were also recorded. Under the optimal excitation wavelength at 270 nm, BA-Eu-MOF exhibited two characteristic peaks at 595 and 620 nm (Fig. S3B), respectively, corresponding to the 5D0-7F1 (595 nm) and 5D0-7F2 (620 nm) transitions of Eu(III) ions. Under 270 nm excitation, the quantum yield (QY) of BA-Eu-MOF was 10.2 % and the decay time was 0.505 ms at 620 nm. The stability of BA-Eu-MOF was also studied. Fig. S3C showed that the emission signal changed slightly after stored for 15 days in aqueous solution, demonstrating the excellent day-to-day fluorescence stability. The good fluorescence stability of BA-Eu-MOF enables the design of fluorescence sensor in aqueous samples. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were used to validate the porosity of BA-Eu-MOF (Fig. S3D). The gas adsorption behavior of BA-Eu-MOF exhibited typical type IV of mesoporous materials with a BET surface area of 5.5166 m2/g. All these results demonstrated the successful preparation of BA-Eu-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from their aglycones
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the discrimination ability of BA-Eu-MOF towards flavonoid glycosides and their aglycones, six flavonoid glycosides and their aglycones were incubated with BA-Eu-MOF, respectively. Fig. 2 A showed that BA-Eu-MOF had an emission at 370 nm originating from 5-bop and four characteristic peaks at 590–710 nm corresponding to the emission of Eu3+ ions owing to the incomplete energy transfer from 5-bop to Eu3+ [33,36]. Among them, the emission at 620 nm was the strongest, which was selected for sensing study. After the addition of flavonoid glycosides including Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut, the emission intensity at 620 nm decreased obviously. In contrast, their aglycones including baicalein, wogonin, kaemperol, daidzein, and quercetin did not induce the fluorescence changes of BA-Eu-MOF (Fig. 2B), although baicalein and quercetin possessed phenolic hydroxyl group of benzene ring. Specially, both Pue and daidzein possessed an intrinsic emission at 476 nm. We deduced that boric acid bonded with cis-diols from sugar more facilely rather than phenolic hydroxyl group on benzene ring.

To verify this hypothesis, we investigated the reaction products of 5-bop with flavonoid glycosides by FT-IR spectra and ESI-MS. Fig. S4 indicated that the peak of -B(OH)2 (1377 cm−1) exhibited blue-shift after incubation with flavonoid glycosides, indicating the formation of a covalent bond between -B(OH)2 and cis-diols group of flavonoid glycosides. Furthermore, Figs. S5–S10 presented that molecular ion signals were obtained at 575.3 (m/z, [M-COOH]-), 622.8 (m/z, [M+H]+), 634.9 (m/z, [M+H]+), 622.8 (m/z, [M+H]+), 588.9 (m/z, [M − H]-) and 740.7 (m/z, [M-COOH]-), which matched well with the molecular weight of the reaction products between 5-bop with Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue, and Rut, respectively. In contrast, baicalin and quercetin were also chosen to react with 5-bop because they possessed phenolic hydroxyl groups on benzene ring. No molecular ion signals corresponding to their reaction products were observed, demonstrating that 5-bop could not coordinate with baicalein and quercetin. To confirm the coordination of BA-Eu-MOF with cis-diols from sugars of flavonoid glycosides, the reaction products of 5-bop with glucuronic acid, glucose, rhamnose and rutinose were characterized by ESI-MS. As presented in Figs. S11–14, molecular ion signals were observed at 391.1 (m/z, [M+Na]+), 377.2 (m/z, [M+Na]+), 337.0 (m/z, [M − H]-) and 501.0 (m/z, [M+H]+), corresponding to the reaction products of 5-bop with glucuronic acid, glucose, rhamnose and rutinose, respectively. Therefore, we concluded that BA-Eu-MOF bonded with cis-diols from sugars of flavonoid glycosides more facilely. All the above results demonstrate that the BA-Eu-MOF holds great potential to discriminate flavonoid glycosides from their aglycones and can be used to detect flavonoid glycosides.

Prior to analysis, the test conditions should be optimized. Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F–F 0)/F 0, where F and F 0 represented the fluorescence intensity of BA-Eu-MOF without and with flavonoid glycosides, respectively, was used as signal output. Firstly, the reaction time between BA-Eu-MOF and flavonoid glycosides were investigated with the results presented in Fig. 3 A. The (F–F 0)/F 0 increased remarkably and kept stable after 5 min, indicating the quick response of BA-Eu-MOF towards flavonoid glycosides. So, 5 min was chosen as the reaction time.

pH is a key factor that influence the coordination affinity between the boric acid and cis-diols [39,40]. Therefore, the influences of pH on the responses of BA-Eu-MOF towards flavonoid glycosides were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, Pue exhibited strong (F–F 0)/F 0 towards BA-Eu-MOF in the whole pH range of 3.5–9.5. For both Bai and Wog, the highest (F–F 0)/F 0 was obtained at pH of 6.5–8.5. For Que, Ast and Rut, the (F–F 0)/F 0 was lower at pH < 6.5 while that became noticeable and remained unchanged at pH ≥ 7.5. Although the responses of BA-Eu-MOF towards these flavonoid glycosides showed a different pH dependence, a basic condition was needed due to the release proton during the formation of boronate complexes. Considering the pH of human physiological fluids, a weak basic condition (pH 7.5) was used.

At last, the amount of BA-Eu-MOF was optimized. Fig. 3C showed that the intensity enhanced gradually as BA-Eu-MOF amount increased. For Pue, the (F–F 0)/F 0 increased remarkably firstly as BA-Eu-MOF amount increased and then became constant when the amount of BA-Eu-MOF reached 50 μg/mL (Fig. 3D). For the other five flavonoid glycosides, the (F–F 0)/F 0 changed slightly when the amount of BA-Eu-MOF increased from 12.5 μg/mL to 150 μg/mL. Consequently, 50 μg/mL BA-Eu-MOF was appropriate for sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of flavonoid glycosides with BA-Eu-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performances of BA-Eu-MOF towards six kinds of flavonoid glycosides were investigated. Upon addition of Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut into the BA-Eu-MOF solutions, the fluorescence intensity decreased as their concentrations increased from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 4 ). Taking Bai as an example, (F–F 0)/F 0 at 620 nm showed a good linear relationship versus Bai concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1 μM and from 1 to 50 μM with linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.996 and 0.997 (Fig. 5 A), respectively. Similarly, (F–F 0)/F 0 showed a good linear relationship versus the other five glycosides (Fig. 5B–F), respectively. Notably, all correlation coefficient of standard curves were larger than 0.994, demonstrating the good linearity. The limit of detections (LODs, S/κ = 3) for Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut were calculated to be 3.3 nM, 3.5 nM, 33 nM, 56 nM, 5.1 nM and 5.5 nM, respectively. Compared with other reported methods, our method possessed the merits of higher sensitivity, wider detect range and lower LOD (Table 1 ).

To evaluate the selectivity of BA-Eu-MOF towards flavonoid glycosides, the effects of a series of possible interferences in human blood including metal ions, anions, amino acids and other biological molecules were tested. Fig. 6 presented that 50 μM flavonoid glycosides induced remarkable fluorescence quenching efficiency while (F–F 0)/F 0 changed slightly in the presence of 50 μM cysteine (Cys), lysine (Lys), serine (Ser), histidine (His), arginine (Arg), threonine (Thr), glutathione (GSH), alanine (Ala), tryptophan (Trp), glutamic acid (Gly), tyrosine (Tyr), citric acid (CA), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), maltose (Mal), sucrose (Suc), Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2− and ascorbic acid (AA). A slight fluorescence quenching was caused by 50 μM AA. However, 25 μM AA hardly changed the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Notably, 50 μM Glu did not induce obvious fluorescence quenching although 5-bop could coordinate with the cis-diols from Glu via covalent interactions. Considering the high concentration of Glu in blood, the effects of Glu with high level on the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF was also tested. As presented in Fig. S15, Glu even with 5 mM did not induce obvious fluorescence change of BA-Eu-MOF. The possible reason was the lack of energy/electronic changes because Glu contained neither chromogenic nor electron withdrawing groups. These results revealed the high selectivity of BA-Eu-MOF towards glycosides and the potential application of BA-Eu-MOF in human blood samples.

Generally speaking, the decrease of “antenna” effect from the ligand leads to the fluorescence quenching of Eu-MOF [33,36]. To verify this hypothesis, the effects of six glycosides on the emission behavior of Eu-MOF were investigated. As presented in Fig. 2A, the introduction of five flavonoid glycosides including Bai, Wog, Ast, Pue, and Rut, the emission of 5-bop at 370 nm enhanced while the intensity from Eu3+ ions at 620 nm decreased obviously. The possible reason may be that the binding of cis-diols structure of these flavonoid glycosides to boron perturbed the Pπ-π conjunction between boron and aromatic chromophore, thus decreasing the intersystem efficiency [33,36]. However, the enhancement for the emission of 5-bop was not significant. Furthermore, the addition of Que did not cause the increase of intensity from 5-bop although it induced the decrease of intensity from Eu3+ ions. Therefore, other fluorescence quenching mechanism may coexist simultaneously.

Common quenching mechanism include PET, IFE and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Fig. 7 A showed that each flavonoid glycoside exhibited strong absorption ranging from 220 to 320 nm, which greatly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of BA-Eu-MOF. In addition, the fluorescence decay curves of BA-Eu-MOF were recorded in the presence of six glycosides. Fig. 7B showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of BA-Eu-MOF without and with Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue, and Rut were 505 μs, 500 μs, 494 μs, 500 μs, 497 μs, 494 μs and 493 μs, respectively. The almost constant fluorescence lifetime further supported the occurrence of IFE between BA-Eu-MOF and six flavonoid glycosides. To verify the PET hypothesis, the molecular orbital energy levels of ligand (5-bop) and glycosides were obtained using density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-31G∗. As presented in Fig. 7C, the LUMO energy levels of six flavonoid glycosides were higher than that of 5-bop ligand, which made it possible to transfer electron from the LUMO of flavonoid glycosides to the LUMO of BA-Eu-MOF, inducing the fluorescence quenching. Therefore, flavonoid glycosides induced the fluorescence quenching of BA-Eu-MOF at 620 nm through decreasing the intersystem efficiency, IFE and PET.

To further evaluate the practical application of this method, flavonoid glycosides in biological samples were detected. In view of the similar responses of BA-Eu-MOF towards these six flavonoid glycosides, we took Bai as an example to demonstrate the applicability of BA-Eu-MOF probe in human blood samples. The responses of BA-Eu-MOF towards Bai in human blood samples were recorded (Fig. S16). As the Bai concentrations increased, the emission intensities at 620 nm decreased gradually (Fig. S16A). Two linear relationships were found in the concentration ranging from 0.05 to 1 μM and 1–40 μM (Fig. S16B), respectively. The linear relationships (y = 0.0232 + 0.0878x, x = 0.05–1 μM) and y = 0.0965 + 0.0135x, x = 1–40 μM) were similar with those in buffer (y = 0.0125 + 0.102x, x = 0.01–1 μM) and y = 0.109 + 0.0132x, x = 1–50 μM), suggesting that the probes could detect flavonoid glycosides in human blood.

Recovery study was further used to validate the performance of this method for flavonoid glycosides. After spiked with standard flavonoid glycosides with different concentrations, the blood samples were analyzed with this method. Table 2 showed that the recoveries of Bai, Que, Wog, Ast and Pue in human blood samples were 96.3–106.0 %, 95.0–104.2 %, 94.7–105.2 %, 96.2–105.5 % and 96.0–106.1 %, respectively. In addition, the detection results obtained with this method presented good agreement with those of HPLC, confirming this nanoprobe can successfully detect flavonoid glycosides in blood.

The method was then employed to analyze rutin in commercialized Rutin Tablets, which was labeled to contain 20 mg rutin per tablet. The rutin content in commercialized rutin tablet was found to be 2.1 μM with this method, which was well matching with the actual rutin content (2.0 μM). Standard rutin solution were spiked into the real samples and the recovery test was performed. Table 2 showed that satisfactory recoveries with good precision were obtained and the detection results obtained with this method were close to those of HPLC, which double confirmed the accuracy of this method.

To summarize, we have developed a fluorescence strategy using BA-Eu-MOF probe for selective recognition of flavonoid glycosides including Bai, Que, Wog, Ast, Pue and Rut to mask the interferences of their corresponding aglycones. Cis-diols of flavonoid glycosides from sugars covalently bonded to hydroxyl group of boric acid group to form cyclic boronic esters, which quenched the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF through decreasing intersystem efficiency, IFE and PET. In contrast, aglycones could not alter the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF because of no covalent bond between them. Furthermore, although they could bond to boric acid group, the sugars including glucose could not alter the fluorescence of BA-Eu-MOF due to lack of sufficient electronic or energy changes. The unique recognition ability of boric acid group enables this method high specificity towards flavonoid glycosides detection without the interference of various potential substances. Furthermore, the satisfactory results of flavonoid glycosides detection in real samples demonstrated the practical application of BA-Eu-MOF probe in complex environment. By comparison with other fluorescent probes for sensing flavonoid glycosides, this probe exhibited advantages of the capability for discrimination of flavonoid glycosides from their corresponding aglycones, excellent selectivity and higher sensitivity. This work provides a highly efficient mean for monitoring flavonoid glycosides as well as extends the application of MOF in pharmaceutical field.

Shuyun Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Kunming Sun: Methodology, Data curation. Shuyi Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Qian Wang: Software. Jiatong Fan: Data curation. Xian-En Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Guobao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22076097 and 22176109) and the Department of Science and Techniques of Jilin Province (No. 20230505016 ZP).
